result.markdown:
---
title: "Module 6: S3 and R Formulas"
format:
  revealjs:
    toc: false
---






## Learning goals

1. Explain what S3 is and give an example of an S3 method
1. Understand the R help pages for S3 methods
1. Understand when and how to use the R formula syntax
1. Use formula syntax to write basic linear and generalized linear models
1. Use common S3 generics to inspect model objects for a report

# Part 1: S3 stands for 3/10 Scariness

## Objects and classes and methods {.incremental}

- We've talked about imperative programming and functional programming. Now we'll talk about **object-oriented programming.**
- S3 is a "functional OOP" system, which is different from OOP you might have dealt with in Python or C++.
- The main property of S3 is **polymorphism**: one **generic function** can act differently on different **classes** of objects, but symbolically represents the same thing and has a consistent interface.

## An S3 example









- We'll use three different model objects to see how one generic function behaves.
- `summary()` is an example of an S3 **generic**. And the documentation tells us this.

```r
?summary
```

## Polymorphism of `summary()`

- When we call `summary(an_object)` it determines what it should do based on the class of `an_object`.
- Each of the following summaries shows us different statistics.

## `summary()` for an `lm`




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
class(an_lm)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "lm"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
coef(summary(an_lm))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


|            |   Estimate| Std. Error|   t value| Pr(>|t|)|
|:-----------|----------:|----------:|---------:|------------------:|
|(Intercept) | 2655.74447|  238.85709| 11.118550|          0.0000000|
|age         |   12.42971|   10.02278|  1.240146|          0.2164752|
:::
:::




## `summary()` for a `glm`




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
class(a_glm)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "glm" "lm" 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
coef(summary(a_glm))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


|            |   Estimate| Std. Error|    z value| Pr(>|z|)|
|:-----------|----------:|----------:|----------:|------------------:|
|(Intercept) |  0.3845819|  0.7321248|  0.5252956|          0.5993777|
|age         | -0.0511529|  0.0315138| -1.6231937|          0.1045480|
:::
:::




## `summary()` for a mixed model




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
class(a_glmm)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "glmerMod"
attr(,"package")
[1] "lme4"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
coef(summary(a_glmm))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


|            |   Estimate| Std. Error|   z value| Pr(>|z|)|
|:-----------|----------:|----------:|---------:|------------------:|
|(Intercept) |  0.3854014|  0.7353431|  0.524111|          0.6002013|
|age         | -0.0512689|  0.0323364| -1.585484|          0.1128564|
:::
:::




## Some OOP definitions

- A **class** is a category of object. Objects of the same class have the same **fields** or attributes.
- A **generic** is a function that has different behavior for multiple classes of inputs.
- The **methods** for a function are the function implementations for specific classes.

## Some OOP definitions, continued

- **Method dispatch** is the process where you call a generic function, the generic function checks the class of the input, and the generic function calls the method for that class.
- A **default method** is dispatched by a generic function when there is no specific method for the class of the input. Not every generic has a default method.

## S3 classes {.incremental}

- Everything that exists in R is an object and has a **type**.
- But not everything is an S3 object, and only S3 objects have a **class**.
- This is confusing, because the function `class()` will tell you something for every object, no matter what. **If an object does not have a class, `class()` will return the type.**
- To check if an object *has* an S3 class, you have to check the extremely confusing `attr(an_object, "class")` instead.

## S3 classes (and types)

- These objects have types but not classes: `NULL`, vectors, lists, functions, and some other special ones that you won't use.
- You can pretty much pretend that types are classes.

## S3 classes (and types)




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
an_object <- 1:10
typeof(an_object)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "integer"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
class(an_object)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "integer"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
attr(an_object, "class")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
NULL
```


:::
:::




## S3 classes (and types)




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
typeof(an_lm)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "list"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
class(an_lm)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "lm"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
attr(an_lm, "class")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "lm"
```


:::
:::




## S3 classes (and types): model objects

- Many S3 objects you will meet in modeling, including `lm` and `glm` objects, are lists with a class attribute.
- Some S3 objects are built on other types, like factors and dates, so do not use "is it a list?" as the definition of S3.
- Complicated model objects are usually lists where the elements have specific names.

## S3 classes (and types): model object names




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
class(an_lm)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "lm"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat(head(names(an_lm), 8), sep = "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
coefficients
residuals
effects
rank
fitted.values
assign
qr
df.residual
```


:::
:::




## S3 classes (and types): help page

- Help pages for modeling functions often document those list elements.

```r
?lm
```

## S3 help pages

- Now you know enough to understand the help pages for S3 objects.

```r
?summary
```

## Finding methods for a generic

- Use the `methods()` function to find all methods that are available for a specific generic function.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summary_methods <- methods(summary)
length(summary_methods)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 88
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat(head(summary_methods, 8), sep = "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
summary,ANY-method
summary,diagonalMatrix-method
summary,sparseMatrix-method
summary.allFit
summary.aov
summary.aovlist
summary.aspell
summary.check_packages_in_dir
```


:::
:::




## Finding methods for a class

- Also use the `methods(class = ...)` function to find all methods that are available for a specific class.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
methods(class = "lm")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 [1] add1           alias          anova          case.names     coerce        
 [6] confint        cooks.distance deviance       dfbeta         dfbetas       
[11] dffits         drop1          dummy.coef     effects        extractAIC    
[16] family         formula        hatvalues      influence      initialize    
[21] kappa          labels         logLik         model.frame    model.matrix  
[26] nobs           plot           predict        print          proj          
[31] qqnorm         qr             residuals      rstandard      rstudent      
[36] show           simulate       slotsFromS3    summary        variable.names
[41] vcov          
see '?methods' for accessing help and source code
```


:::
:::




## Opening a specific method

- You can use a dot (`.`) to reference specific methods, not just generics.

```r
?summary.lm
```


# Part 2: Formulas / Formulae

## Utility of S3 methods

- Hopefully you can now see why understanding S3 methods is important.
- But if you aren't convinced, maybe `formula` objects can convince you. Look how many methods there are!




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
formula_methods <- methods(class = "formula")
length(formula_methods)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 46
```


:::
:::




## Utility of S3 methods: examples




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
cat(head(formula_methods, 4), sep = "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[.formula
aggregate.formula
alias.formula
all.equal.formula
```


:::
:::




- And that doesn't include functions like `lm()` which ONLY have methods for formulas, not generics.

## Formula example

- Say you want to get the correlation between MCV1 coverage rate and measles cases in Pakistan. You can call the `cor.test()` function by passing two vectors.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
meas <- readr::read_rds(here::here("data", "measles_final.Rds"))

vector_cor <- cor.test(
	x = meas$MCV1_coverage[meas$iso3c == "PAK"],
	y = meas$measles_cases[meas$iso3c == "PAK"]
)
```
:::




## Formula example: vector result




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
cat(sprintf(
	"estimate: %.3f\np-value: %.3g\n",
	unname(vector_cor$estimate),
	vector_cor$p.value
))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
estimate: -0.447
p-value: 0.00263
```


:::
:::




## Formula example: methods

- But if we inspect the methods for `cor.test`, we see it is an S3 generic with a method for `formula` class objects.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
methods(cor.test)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] cor.test.default* cor.test.formula*
see '?methods' for accessing help and source code
```


:::
:::




## Formula example: formula method

- Typing in the code is easier, and reading the output is easier.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
formula_cor <- cor.test(
	~ measles_cases + MCV1_coverage,
	data = subset(meas, iso3c == "PAK")
)

cat(sprintf(
	"estimate: %.3f\np-value: %.3g\n",
	unname(formula_cor$estimate),
	formula_cor$p.value
))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
estimate: -0.447
p-value: 0.00263
```


:::
:::




- But it pays to understand S3, because in certain circumstances, using the default method is easier!

## Writing formulas {.incremental}

- The formula language is a smaller language inside of R used for writing statistical models, and the full details [are in the `?formula` documentation](https://stat.ethz.ch/R-manual/R-devel/library/stats/html/formula.html).
- This little formula language is so useful it is now used in many textbooks and other languages (Python and Julia).
- The basic syntax is `dv ~ iv1 + iv2 + ...`.
- Recall that in `cor.test()` our formula didn't have a LHS -- there is no dependent variable in correlation.

## You try it!

**Student prompt**

- Before, we saw that `boxplot` had a formula method. Use `boxplot()` with a formula to make boxplots of measles cases in 2002 for each `region` in the dataset.

## You try it: hint

**Hint**

Your code should look like this for the correct DV and IV.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
boxplot(dv ~ iv, data = subset(meas, year == 2002))
```
:::




## You try it: solution

**Solution**




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
boxplot(measles_cases ~ region, data = subset(meas, year == 2002))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Module06-S3-lm-formulas_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::




## You try it (more advanced)

**Student prompt**

- Load the QCRC dataset, you only need the sheet "Main_Dataset" for this.
- Fit a logistic regression model with 30 day mortality (`30D_Mortality` in the spreadsheet) as the outcome. Include any continuous or categorical predictors you want, we recommend avoiding variables that are (supposed to be) date/times.
- Use appropriate S3 generic functions to get statistics about your model that you would want to report.

## You try it (more advanced): report pattern

- This is the same formula and model-inspection pattern you will use in the Exercise 2 QCRC report.

## You try it (more advanced): read the data

**Hint**

To read in the data, do this. You might need to do some data cleaning, because missing data are written as a period in the spreadsheet.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qcrc <- readxl::read_xlsx(
	here::here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx"),
	sheet = "Main_Dataset"
)
```
:::




## You try it (more advanced): model hint

**Hint**

Your logistic regression code should look like this. You need to put backticks (this thing: \`, NOT an apostrophe. It's made by the ~ key on American keyboards.) around the name of the variable \`30D_Mortality\`.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
model_30dm <- glm(
	something ~ something + something + ...,
	data = qcrc,
	family = "binomial"
)
```
:::




## You try it (more advanced): method hint

**Hint**

Some useful functions with methods for `glm`-class objects are `summary`, `coef`, and `confint`. (You probably also need the function `exp`!)

## You try it (more advanced): solution setup

**Solution**

One solution, you could have used any predictors you want, though you should be able to explain why they might affect 30 day mortality.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# A little cleaning for D Dimer
# First turn the "." into actual NA's and make it a real number
qcrc$d_dimer <- as.numeric(dplyr::na_if(qcrc$`D dimer`, "."))
# Take the log cause it's skewed and positive
qcrc$d_dimer <- log(qcrc$d_dimer)
# Impute missing values with the median -- there are much better ways to do this
med_d_dimer <- median(qcrc$d_dimer, na.rm = TRUE)
qcrc$d_dimer <- ifelse(is.na(qcrc$d_dimer), med_d_dimer, qcrc$d_dimer)
```
:::




## You try it (more advanced): solution setup, continued




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# A little cleaning for Race
qcrc$Race <- factor(qcrc$Race) |>
	# Make White the reference level
	relevel(ref = "Caucasian or White") |>
	# Groups other than "Caucasian or White" and "Black or African American" are
	# really small and heterogeneous so put them together
	# Not ideal but sometimes the best we can do
	forcats::fct_lump_n(n = 2)
```
:::




## You try it (more advanced): solution model




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Fit a model that controls for treatment, d_dimer, and race/ethnicity
model_30dm <- glm(
	`30D_Mortality` ~ Remdesivir_or_placebo + d_dimer + Race,
	data = qcrc,
	family = "binomial"
)
```
:::




## You try it (more advanced): model summary

- Look at a lot of useful information with `summary()`.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
model_summary <- round(coef(summary(model_30dm)), 3)
```
:::




## You try it (more advanced): model summary table




::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}


|                               | Estimate| Std. Error| z value| Pr(>|z|)|
|:------------------------------|--------:|----------:|-------:|------------------:|
|(Intercept)                    |   -2.032|      0.788|  -2.580|              0.010|
|Remdesivir_or_placebo          |   -0.811|      0.387|  -2.097|              0.036|
|d_dimer                        |    0.165|      0.099|   1.662|              0.096|
|RaceAfrican American  or Black |    0.118|      0.348|   0.339|              0.735|
|RaceOther                      |    0.577|      0.465|   1.239|              0.215|


:::
:::




## You try it (more advanced): odds ratios

- Get the OR's with `coef()`.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
coef(model_30dm) |>
	exp() |>
	round(2)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                   (Intercept)          Remdesivir_or_placebo 
                          0.13                           0.44 
                       d_dimer RaceAfrican American  or Black 
                          1.18                           1.13 
                     RaceOther 
                          1.78 
```


:::
:::




## You try it (more advanced): confidence intervals

- Get the profile CI's with `confint()`.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
model_ci <- suppressMessages(confint(model_30dm)) |>
	exp() |>
	round(2)
```
:::




## You try it (more advanced): confidence interval table




::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}


|                               | 2.5 %| 97.5 %|
|:------------------------------|-----:|------:|
|(Intercept)                    |  0.03|   0.61|
|Remdesivir_or_placebo          |  0.20|   0.92|
|d_dimer                        |  0.97|   1.44|
|RaceAfrican American  or Black |  0.58|   2.28|
|RaceOther                      |  0.71|   4.47|


:::
:::




## You try it (more advanced): interpretation

- A basic interpretation of the coefficient for `Remdesivir_or_placebo` (coded as 1 for remdesivir and 0 for placebo) might be "Odds of mortality after 30 days in the remdesivir group were 0.48 times the odds in the placebo group, for patients with the same D dimer level and race/ethnicity. The 95% CI for the odds ratio was 0.20 to 1.04. The effect was not significant at the $\alpha = 0.05$ significance level, but is suggestive that at least some patients benefitted from remdesivir treatment."

## Summary

- We learned the basics of "functional OOP" and specifically R's S3 system, including how to check classes and find S3 methods for a class or classes for an S3 method.
- We learned the basics of R formulas, a consistent and powerful sub-language in R for writing statistical models.
- There's a lot more to formulas, but we'll discuss them more in a separate module based on interest/time.
- If you want to read more about S3 or OOP in R, check out [the OOP section in Wickham's Advanced R](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/oo.html).

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
